segments.text: [music]
Let's explore audit logs now available
on sonarcubecloud. We made this
announcement back on October 1st in our
sonar community forum available at
community.source.com.
I highly recommend you log in and click
on the bell icon to be notified of any
new announcements
and features.
In the audit logs documentation
available at docs.sonsource.com,
you'll find a list of the logged events
which are necessary for
compliance or security needs. This is
accessed only through the audit logs
API, not through the UI, at least not
yet.
To access these logs, you'll first need
to authenticate with web API and then go
to the audit logs API.
Here on this documentation page, you'll
see that you need the authorization
bearer token header.
So once you click the audit logs API, it
will take you to our swagger docs.
Initially, it'll look like this.
and select download. Notice that the
first parameter necessary is the
enterprise UID. If you don't know that,
you'll need to access it through this
other API list enterprises.
Select your server
and select a domain. I'll choose
ThunderCloud.io
and select the first API to get your
enterprises.
We'll click try it out and type the
enterprise key. If you don't know your
enterprise key, go to your enterprise
and sonar cloud io
and you'll see it in the URL. In my
case, it's sonar support team-sqc.
I'll copy that and go back to the
swagger docs, paste it, and execute.
Here I see an example of the API I need
to submit to get my enterprise UU ID.
I'll use Postman as an example of how to
get that information.
First, I'll set my user token obtained
from SonarCloud.io
bear token off type enterprise key param
with my enterprise key
and send
here. This ID is the UU ID of your
enterprise.
Let's go back to Swagger docs.
Now we can download the audit logs.
Again, make sure you've chosen the right
domain. Son, sonocloud.io.
Choose /d download. We're going to try
it out. Let's paste in that UU ID we got
here.
Let's choose a start date
October 11th with the end date of
November 10th.
Notice that the date range cannot exceed
30 days. You can optionally provide a
page number or a maximum number of
records per page size or rather per
page. execute.
And here's an example of the API you
need to submit to get the audit logs.
I'm going to use Postman once again.
Here's the URL that Swagger has given
me.
I have set my bearer token off type with
my token. I'll click send
here. Postman neatly formats it into
JSON
as pretty print.
You can then save the response to a file
or save the response within Postman
and then you have a JSON file of your
audit logs. Now, how would you use this
in a SIM tool like Splunk or Data Dog?
Let's do an example with Splunk Cloud.
Let's upload that JSON file we just
created. So, we'll go to settings, add
data,
upload files from my computer,
and I'll select my file.
We'll click next.
You can optionally change the source
type and other factors.
input settings and review. Click submit
to finish and start searching.
And there you have your results in a
singular JSON file.
Now, this isn't necessarily the
prettiest method to see the data.
Instead,
let's clean this data up,
remove it from our search.
Let's try uploading a formatted version
of our audit logs.
So going back to this, I've downloaded
my logs and I have prepared a formatted
version using a Python script that will
set the event host
source type and source fields that
Splunk typically uses with HTTP event
collector. First,
let's get an HTTP event collector token.
We'll go to settings, add data, and
select monitor.
We'll choose HTTP event collector and
give a name.
I leave the defaults.
Submit. Done. So here, notice the token
value. This token is necessary so that
Splunk understands where that data is
coming from and what for what purpose.
You can then use curl to submit that
data. Here's an example of me uploading
that data using curl to the services
collector event endpoint using my Splunk
token.
Success.
I'll go back and start searching.
My source type was called audit JSON.
date range was back between October and
November.
Search and here we are.
This is a bit better at formatting.
Now that makes searching for your audit
logs much easier and prettier. For more
information, make sure to check out the
audit logs portion of our documentation
in the Suncube Cloud product
documentation.

fullText: [music] Let's explore audit logs now available on sonarcubecloud. We made this announcement back on October 1st in our sonar community forum available at community.source.com. I highly recommend you log in and click on the bell icon to be notified of any new announcements and features. In the audit logs documentation available at docs.sonsource.com, you'll find a list of the logged events which are necessary for compliance or security needs. This is accessed only through the audit logs API, not through the UI, at least not yet. To access these logs, you'll first need to authenticate with web API and then go to the audit logs API. Here on this documentation page, you'll see that you need the authorization bearer token header. So once you click the audit logs API, it will take you to our swagger docs. Initially, it'll look like this. and select download. Notice that the first parameter necessary is the enterprise UID. If you don't know that, you'll need to access it through this other API list enterprises. Select your server and select a domain. I'll choose ThunderCloud.io and select the first API to get your enterprises. We'll click try it out and type the enterprise key. If you don't know your enterprise key, go to your enterprise and sonar cloud io and you'll see it in the URL. In my case, it's sonar support team-sqc. I'll copy that and go back to the swagger docs, paste it, and execute. Here I see an example of the API I need to submit to get my enterprise UU ID. I'll use Postman as an example of how to get that information. First, I'll set my user token obtained from SonarCloud.io bear token off type enterprise key param with my enterprise key and send here. This ID is the UU ID of your enterprise. Let's go back to Swagger docs. Now we can download the audit logs. Again, make sure you've chosen the right domain. Son, sonocloud.io. Choose /d download. We're going to try it out. Let's paste in that UU ID we got here. Let's choose a start date October 11th with the end date of November 10th. Notice that the date range cannot exceed 30 days. You can optionally provide a page number or a maximum number of records per page size or rather per page. execute. And here's an example of the API you need to submit to get the audit logs. I'm going to use Postman once again. Here's the URL that Swagger has given me. I have set my bearer token off type with my token. I'll click send here. Postman neatly formats it into JSON as pretty print. You can then save the response to a file or save the response within Postman and then you have a JSON file of your audit logs. Now, how would you use this in a SIM tool like Splunk or Data Dog? Let's do an example with Splunk Cloud. Let's upload that JSON file we just created. So, we'll go to settings, add data, upload files from my computer, and I'll select my file. We'll click next. You can optionally change the source type and other factors. input settings and review. Click submit to finish and start searching. And there you have your results in a singular JSON file. Now, this isn't necessarily the prettiest method to see the data. Instead, let's clean this data up, remove it from our search. Let's try uploading a formatted version of our audit logs. So going back to this, I've downloaded my logs and I have prepared a formatted version using a Python script that will set the event host source type and source fields that Splunk typically uses with HTTP event collector. First, let's get an HTTP event collector token. We'll go to settings, add data, and select monitor. We'll choose HTTP event collector and give a name. I leave the defaults. Submit. Done. So here, notice the token value. This token is necessary so that Splunk understands where that data is coming from and what for what purpose. You can then use curl to submit that data. Here's an example of me uploading that data using curl to the services collector event endpoint using my Splunk token. Success. I'll go back and start searching. My source type was called audit JSON. date range was back between October and November. Search and here we are. This is a bit better at formatting. Now that makes searching for your audit logs much easier and prettier. For more information, make sure to check out the audit logs portion of our documentation in the Suncube Cloud product documentation.
